category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Statistics and Probability/Testing of Hypotheses

info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Testing of Hypotheses: Neyman-Pearson lemma, UMP tests, likelihood ratio tests, chi-square tests, t-tests, F-tests, non-parametric tests, p-values, power functions.

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(\mu, 1)\). Testing \(H_0: \mu = 0\) vs \(H_1: \mu = 1\) at level \(\alpha\). By the Neyman-Pearson lemma, the most powerful test rejects \(H_0\) when \(\Lambda = f_1(\mathbf{x})/f_0(\mathbf{x}) > k\). The most powerful rejection region is:

feedback.text: The likelihood ratio \(\Lambda = e^{n\bar{x} - n/2}\). The test rejects when \(\bar{X} > k'\) for some constant. Under \(H_0\), \(\sqrt{n}\bar{X} \sim N(0,1)\), so the critical value is \(z_\alpha/\sqrt{n}\) where \(z_\alpha\) is the upper \(\alpha\) quantile of \(N(0,1)\).
This two-sided test is appropriate for \(H_1: \mu \neq 0\), not for the one-sided \(H_1: \mu = 1\). The NP lemma gives a one-sided test here.
Wrong direction. Since \(H_1: \mu=1 > 0\), larger \(\bar{X}\) is evidence against \(H_0\), so we reject for large positive \(\bar{X}\).
The chi-square test statistic arises for variance testing, not for testing the normal mean.

name.text: STAT22-Q02 UMP Test for Exponential Family

questiontext.text: For \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} \text{Exp}(\lambda)\), testing \(H_0: \lambda \leq \lambda_0\) vs \(H_1: \lambda > \lambda_0\). A UMP level-\(\alpha\) test exists because the family has a monotone likelihood ratio in \(T = \sum X_i\). The UMP test rejects \(H_0\) when:

answer.text: \(\sum X_i < c\) where \(c\) is determined by \(P_{\lambda_0}(\sum X_i < c) = \alpha\)

feedback.text: Larger \(\lambda\) (shorter mean lifetime) corresponds to smaller \(\sum X_i\). The MLR in \(\sum X_i\) is decreasing in \(\lambda\), so reject for small \(\sum X_i\). Under \(H_0:\lambda=\lambda_0\), \(2\lambda_0\sum X_i \sim \chi^2_{2n}\), giving the critical value.

answer.text: \(\sum X_i > c\) where \(c\) is the upper \(\alpha\) quantile

feedback.text: Larger \(\lambda\) means smaller mean \(1/\lambda\), hence smaller \(\sum X_i\). The rejection region should be \(\sum X_i < c\) for large \(\lambda\).
Equivalent to rejecting for large \(\sum X_i\), which is the wrong direction for detecting \(\lambda > \lambda_0\).
A two-sided test loses power for the one-sided alternative. UMP test is one-sided.

questiontext.text: The generalized likelihood ratio test statistic is \(\Lambda = \dfrac{\sup_{\theta \in \Theta_0} L(\theta)}{\sup_{\theta \in \Theta} L(\theta)}\). Under \(H_0\), Wilks' theorem states \(-2\log\Lambda \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_r\) where \(r\) is the number of constraints. For testing \(H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2\) in two independent \(N(\mu_i, \sigma^2)\) samples (with common unknown \(\sigma^2\), total \(n\) observations), what is \(r\)?

feedback.text: The full model has parameters \((\mu_1, \mu_2, \sigma^2)\), dimension 3. The null model \(\mu_1=\mu_2=\mu\) has parameters \((\mu, \sigma^2)\), dimension 2. The number of constraints is \(3-2=1\), so \(-2\log\Lambda \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_1\).
There is only one constraint (\(\mu_1=\mu_2\)), not two. The degrees of freedom equals the number of independent restrictions.
The chi-square degrees of freedom in Wilks' theorem is the number of parameter constraints, not sample size minus parameters.
The full parameter space has dimension 3. But \(r\) is the difference between full and null dimensions, which is 1.

name.text: STAT22-Q04 Type I and Type II Errors

questiontext.text: For testing \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) vs \(H_1: \theta = \theta_1\) at level \(\alpha = 0.05\), a test has power \(\beta(\theta_1) = 0.80\). What is the probability of a Type II error?

feedback.text: Power = \(P(\text{reject }H_0 | H_1\text{ true}) = 1 - P(\text{Type II error}) = 0.80\). So \(P(\text{Type II error}) = \beta = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20\). Type I error = \(\alpha = 0.05\) (probability of rejecting a true \(H_0\)).
This is the Type I error (level \(\alpha\)), not the Type II error. Type II error = 1 - power = 0.20.
This is the power, which equals 1 - Type II error. The Type II error probability is 0.20.
Incorrect. Type II error = 1 - power = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20, not 0.75.

questiontext.text: Two independent samples from \(N(\mu_1,\sigma^2)\) and \(N(\mu_2,\sigma^2)\) (equal variances, unknown) of sizes \(n_1=10, n_2=15\) have \(\bar{X}_1=12, \bar{X}_2=10, S_p^2=9\) (pooled variance). The test statistic for \(H_0: \mu_1=\mu_2\) is \(T = \dfrac{\bar{X}_1-\bar{X}_2}{S_p\sqrt{1/n_1+1/n_2}}\). Under \(H_0\), \(T \sim\):

feedback.text: The pooled two-sample \(t\)-statistic follows \(t_{n_1+n_2-2} = t_{10+15-2} = t_{23}\) under \(H_0\). The degrees of freedom comes from the pooled variance estimate: \((n_1-1)+(n_2-1) = 9+14=23\).
Degrees of freedom for two-sample \(t\)-test is \(n_1+n_2-2 = 23\), not 24.
This would apply only if \(\sigma^2\) were known. Since it's estimated, the distribution is \(t_{23}\).
The chi-square distribution is used for variance testing, not for comparing means.

questiontext.text: A chi-square goodness-of-fit test for a die (6 categories) uses \(n=120\) rolls. Observed frequencies are 18, 22, 19, 21, 20, 20. Under \(H_0\) (fair die), expected frequency is 20 for each. The test statistic is \(\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^6 \dfrac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}\). What is the degrees of freedom for this test?

feedback.text: For a goodness-of-fit test with \(k\) categories and no parameters estimated from data, df = \(k-1 = 6-1 = 5\). Under \(H_0\), \(\chi^2 = \frac{4}{20}+\frac{4}{20}+\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{20}+0+0 = 0.5 \sim \chi^2_5\) approximately.
The df is \(k-1\), not \(k\). One degree of freedom is lost because the observed frequencies sum to \(n\).
4 degrees of freedom would apply if one parameter were estimated from the data. Here, all probabilities are specified under \(H_0\), so df = \(k-1=5\).
This is \(n-1\), appropriate for a continuous distribution test (like KS test), not chi-square goodness-of-fit with 6 categories.

questiontext.text: The \(p\)-value of a test is the probability, under \(H_0\), of observing a test statistic as extreme as or more extreme than the observed value. For a one-sided \(z\)-test with observed statistic \(z_{obs} = 2.1\), the \(p\)-value is \(P(Z \geq 2.1)\) where \(Z \sim N(0,1)\). Using \(\Phi(2.1) \approx 0.982\), the \(p\)-value is approximately:

feedback.text: This is \(\Phi(2.1) = P(Z \leq 2.1)\), not the \(p\)-value. The \(p\)-value for upper-tailed test is \(1-\Phi(2.1)\).
This is the two-sided \(p\)-value: \(2(1-\Phi(2.1)) = 0.036\). The question specifies a one-sided test.
2.1 is the test statistic, not the \(p\)-value. The \(p\)-value is a probability between 0 and 1.

name.text: STAT22-Q08 F-Test for Equality of Variances

questiontext.text: Two independent normal samples: \(X_1,\ldots,X_m \sim N(\mu_1,\sigma_1^2)\) and \(Y_1,\ldots,Y_n \sim N(\mu_2,\sigma_2^2)\). For testing \(H_0:\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2\), the test statistic \(F = S_X^2/S_Y^2\) where \(S_X^2, S_Y^2\) are sample variances. Under \(H_0\), \(F\) follows:

feedback.text: \((m-1)S_X^2/\sigma_1^2 \sim \chi^2_{m-1}\) and \((n-1)S_Y^2/\sigma_2^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-1}\) independently. Under \(H_0\): \(\sigma_1^2=\sigma_2^2\), so \(F = S_X^2/S_Y^2 = \frac{\chi^2_{m-1}/(m-1)}{\chi^2_{n-1}/(n-1)} \sim F_{m-1,n-1}\).
The ratio of independent chi-square variables (divided by df) follows an \(F\) distribution, not chi-square.
The \(t\)-distribution arises for testing means. For variance ratio testing, the \(F\) distribution is appropriate.
The degrees of freedom are \(m-1\) and \(n-1\) (from the chi-square distributions), not \(m\) and \(n\).

questiontext.text: In one-way ANOVA with \(k=4\) groups each of size \(n=6\) (total \(N=24\)), the between-groups sum of squares is \(SS_B = 120\) and within-groups sum of squares is \(SS_W = 180\). The \(F\)-statistic for testing equality of group means is:

feedback.text: Between df is \(k-1=3\), not \(k=4\). Numerator df should be 3.
Within df is \(N-k = 24-4 = 20\), not \(N-1=23\).
The \(F\)-statistic is \(MS_B/MS_W\) (between over within), not the reciprocal.

name.text: STAT22-Q10 Uniformly Most Powerful Unbiased Test

questiontext.text: For \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)\) with \(\sigma^2\) unknown, testing \(H_0: \mu = \mu_0\) vs \(H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0\). No UMP test exists for this two-sided hypothesis. The UMPU test rejects when \(|T| > t_{n-1, \alpha/2}\) where \(T = \dfrac{\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}-\mu_0)}{S}\). The power function of this test is:

answer.text: Symmetric about \(\mu_0\) and increasing as \(|\mu - \mu_0|\) increases

feedback.text: The power function \(\beta(\mu) = P_\mu(|T| > t_{n-1,\alpha/2})\) depends on the noncentrality parameter \(\delta = \sqrt{n}(\mu-\mu_0)/\sigma\). It equals \(\alpha\) at \(\mu=\mu_0\), is symmetric about \(\mu_0\), and increases as \(|\mu-\mu_0|\) increases, approaching 1 as \(\mu\to\pm\infty\).
A monotone power function characterizes one-sided tests. For two-sided alternatives, the power function is U-shaped with minimum at \(\mu_0\).
A constant power equal to \(\alpha\) would mean the test has no power, i.e., is equivalent to random rejection. The actual power increases as \(\mu\) moves away from \(\mu_0\).
This describes an inverted U-shape. The correct shape for a two-sided test is U-shaped (minimum at \(\mu_0\), increasing in both directions).

questiontext.text: A 2×2 contingency table has cell counts \(\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}\) with row totals \(r_1=a+b, r_2=c+d\), column totals \(c_1=a+c, c_2=b+d\), and total \(n=a+b+c+d\). The chi-square statistic for independence \(\chi^2 = \dfrac{n(ad-bc)^2}{r_1 r_2 c_1 c_2}\) under \(H_0\) follows approximately \(\chi^2_1\). What is the expected frequency of cell \((1,1)\) under independence?

feedback.text: Under independence, \(P(\text{row }1, \text{col }1) = P(\text{row }1)\cdot P(\text{col }1) = (r_1/n)(c_1/n)\). So the expected count is \(n \cdot (r_1/n)(c_1/n) = r_1c_1/n\). This is the fundamental formula for expected frequencies in contingency table tests.
This mixes diagonal elements. Expected frequency uses marginal totals: \(r_1 c_1/n\).
This would be the expected count if all cells were equally likely, but the expected count depends on the marginal totals.
Incorrect formula. Expected count is the product of marginals divided by \(n\): \(r_1 c_1/n\).

questiontext.text: In a sign test for \(H_0: m = m_0\) (median equals \(m_0\)) based on \(n=20\) observations, the number of observations exceeding \(m_0\) is \(K = 14\). Under \(H_0\), \(K \sim \text{Bin}(20, 0.5)\). For a two-sided test, the exact \(p\)-value is:

feedback.text: For a two-sided sign test, the \(p\)-value is \(2\min(P(K\geq k), P(K\leq k)) = 2P(K\geq 14)\) by symmetry of \(\text{Bin}(20,0.5)\) about 10. Since \(k=14>10\), we compute \(p = 2P(K\geq14) = 2\sum_{j=14}^{20}\binom{20}{j}(0.5)^{20} \approx 0.115\).
This is the one-sided \(p\)-value. For a two-sided test, we multiply by 2.
The \(p\)-value includes the probability of the observed value and all more extreme values, not just the point probability.
This equals \(P(K\geq 14)\), which is only the one-sided \(p\)-value. For two-sided, double this quantity.

questiontext.text: The Wilcoxon rank-sum (Mann-Whitney) test is a nonparametric alternative to the two-sample \(t\)-test. For two independent samples of sizes \(m\) and \(n\) (\(m \leq n\)), the test statistic \(W\) is the sum of ranks of the smaller sample in the combined ranking. Under \(H_0\) (equal distributions), the mean and variance of \(W\) are:

feedback.text: Under \(H_0\), each rank assignment is equally likely. \(E[W] = m \cdot (m+n+1)/2\) (each rank has expected value \((m+n+1)/2\)), and \(\text{Var}(W) = mn(m+n+1)/12\). For large samples, \(W\) is approximately normal.
Incorrect; missing the factor \((m+n+1)\) in both expressions.
Incorrect formula. The correct expressions involve \(m+n+1\) throughout, not just \(n+1\).
These are incorrect formulas for the Wilcoxon statistic under the null hypothesis.

questiontext.text: In the Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT) of Wald for testing \(H_0: \theta=\theta_0\) vs \(H_1: \theta=\theta_1\), the test continues sampling as long as \(B < \Lambda_n < A\) where \(\Lambda_n = \prod_{i=1}^n f_1(X_i)/f_0(X_i)\). The boundaries are \(A \approx (1-\beta)/\alpha\) and \(B \approx \beta/(1-\alpha)\). If \(\alpha=\beta=0.05\), then approximately:

feedback.text: These are \(z\)-score critical values for normal tests, not SPRT boundaries for the likelihood ratio.

questiontext.text: The Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) one-sample test statistic is \(D_n = \sup_x |F_n(x) - F_0(x)|\) where \(F_n\) is the empirical CDF and \(F_0\) is the hypothesized CDF. A key property: under \(H_0\) (that data comes from \(F_0\) continuous), the distribution of \(D_n\) is:

feedback.text: By the probability integral transform: if \(X \sim F_0\), then \(U = F_0(X) \sim U(0,1)\). The KS statistic based on \(F_0\) has the same distribution as the KS statistic for \(U(0,1)\), regardless of \(F_0\). This distribution-free property makes KS tables universally applicable.

answer.text: Depends on \(F_0\) through its mean and variance

feedback.text: Incorrect. The probability integral transform shows the distribution of \(D_n\) is the same for all continuous \(F_0\).
\(\sqrt{n}D_n\) converges to the Kolmogorov distribution, not the normal distribution. The limiting distribution is the supremum of a Brownian bridge.

answer.text: Chi-square distributed with \(n-1\) degrees of freedom

feedback.text: Chi-square arises in goodness-of-fit tests for discrete/grouped data. The KS test statistic has a different (Kolmogorov) limiting distribution.

questiontext.text: In a paired \(t\)-test, \(n\) pairs \((X_i, Y_i)\) are observed and \(D_i = X_i - Y_i\) are assumed \(N(\mu_D, \sigma_D^2)\). For testing \(H_0: \mu_D = 0\), the test statistic is \(T = \dfrac{\bar{D}\sqrt{n}}{S_D}\) where \(S_D^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum(D_i-\bar{D})^2\). Under \(H_0\), \(T \sim t_{n-1}\). Why is the paired test preferred over the two-sample \(t\)-test for paired data?

answer.text: Pairing eliminates between-subject variability, reducing the error variance and increasing power.

feedback.text: The paired test uses \(\text{Var}(D_i) = \text{Var}(X_i) + \text{Var}(Y_i) - 2\text{Cov}(X_i,Y_i)\). When \(X_i\) and \(Y_i\) are positively correlated (common in before/after designs), the covariance term reduces the variance, giving a smaller standard error and higher power.

answer.text: Pairing always gives a smaller test statistic, making rejection easier.

feedback.text: Pairing reduces error variance but also halves the degrees of freedom (\(n-1\) vs \(2n-2\)). The paired test is only preferred when pairing explains substantial variability.

answer.text: The paired test does not require the normality assumption.

feedback.text: The paired \(t\)-test also requires normality of the differences (or large \(n\) for the CLT). Nonparametric alternatives like the sign test or Wilcoxon signed-rank test can be used without normality.

answer.text: The paired test is always more powerful regardless of correlation structure.

feedback.text: If correlation is zero or negative, pairing doesn't help and may reduce power (due to fewer df). Pairing is beneficial only when within-pair correlation is positive and substantial.

questiontext.text: For testing \(H_0: \theta = \theta_0\) vs \(H_1: \theta > \theta_0\), a locally most powerful (LMP) test at \(\theta_0\) rejects for large values of the score statistic \(S(\theta_0) = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log L(\theta)\Big|_{\theta=\theta_0}\). The LMP test is equivalent to the UMP test when the family has MLR. For the one-parameter exponential family, the LMP test is equivalent to:

answer.text: The UMP test (which is also based on the natural sufficient statistic)

feedback.text: For the exponential family, the score statistic \(S(\theta_0) = \eta'(\theta_0)\sum T(X_i) - nA'(\theta_0)\). Rejecting for large \(S(\theta_0)\) is equivalent to rejecting for large \(\sum T(X_i)\) (the natural sufficient statistic), which is exactly the UMP test by the Karlin-Rubin theorem.
Squaring the score gives the score test for two-sided alternatives. For one-sided, we use \(S(\theta_0)\) directly.
The score, Wald, and LR tests are asymptotically equivalent but not identical. For the exponential family, the LMP (score) test happens to equal the UMP test exactly.

answer.text: The Wald test based on \((\hat{\theta}-\theta_0)^2 I(\hat{\theta})\)

feedback.text: The Wald test uses the MLE, while the score test evaluates at \(\theta_0\). Both are asymptotically equivalent but not identical for finite samples.

questiontext.text: When performing \(m=20\) simultaneous hypothesis tests, each at level \(\alpha_i\), the family-wise error rate (FWER) \(= P(\text{at least one false rejection})\). The Bonferroni correction sets each \(\alpha_i = \alpha/m\) to control FWER at \(\alpha\). For FWER \(\leq 0.05\) with \(m=20\) independent tests, the individual level is:

feedback.text: By Bonferroni: \(\alpha_i = \alpha/m = 0.05/20 = 0.0025\). The Bonferroni bound gives FWER \(= P(\text{at least one error}) \leq \sum_{i=1}^m \alpha_i = m\cdot(\alpha/m) = \alpha = 0.05\) by the union bound.
Testing each hypothesis at 0.05 gives FWER \(\approx 1-(0.95)^{20} \approx 0.64\), far exceeding the target 0.05.
This would correspond to \(m=50\), not 20. For \(m=20\), the Bonferroni level is \(0.05/20 = 0.0025\).
This corresponds to \(m=5\). For \(m=20\), the Bonferroni correction gives \(0.05/20 = 0.0025\).

questiontext.text: The Neyman-Pearson lemma characterizes the most powerful test at level \(\alpha\). For discrete distributions, randomized tests may be needed to achieve exact size \(\alpha\). The randomized NP test rejects with probability \(\phi(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \Lambda > k \\ \gamma & \Lambda = k \\ 0 & \Lambda < k \end{cases}\). The value of \(\gamma\) is chosen so that:

feedback.text: \(\gamma\) is chosen so the test exactly achieves level \(\alpha\): \(P_{H_0}(\Lambda>k) + \gamma P_{H_0}(\Lambda=k) = \alpha\). This gives \(\gamma = [\alpha - P_{H_0}(\Lambda>k)]/P_{H_0}(\Lambda=k)\) when \(0 < \gamma < 1\). The randomization handles the discrete probability mass at the boundary.
\(\gamma\) controls the size of the test under \(H_0\), not the power under \(H_1\). Maximizing power is the result of the lemma, not the condition on \(\gamma\).
\(\gamma\) depends on the specific distribution and the chosen \(\alpha\). It is not always 0.5.

answer.text: The Type II error probability equals \(\alpha\)

feedback.text: \(\gamma\) ensures the Type I error equals \(\alpha\), not that Type I equals Type II.

questiontext.text: The Wald test for \(H_0: g(\theta) = 0\) (where \(g\) is differentiable) uses the statistic \(W_n = \dfrac{g(\hat{\theta}_n)^2}{\widehat{\text{Var}}(g(\hat{\theta}_n))}\) which is asymptotically \(\chi^2_1\) under \(H_0\). By the delta method, \(\text{Var}(g(\hat{\theta})) \approx [g'(\theta)]^2/\{nI(\theta)\}\). For testing \(H_0: e^\theta = 2\) (i.e., \(\theta = \log 2\)) with MLE \(\hat{\theta}\), the asymptotic variance of \(e^{\hat{\theta}}\) is:

feedback.text: Let \(g(\theta) = e^\theta\), so \(g'(\theta) = e^\theta\). By the delta method: \(\text{Var}(e^{\hat{\theta}}) \approx [g'(\theta)]^2 \cdot \text{Var}(\hat{\theta}) = (e^\theta)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{nI(\theta)} = \frac{e^{2\theta}}{nI(\theta)}\).
The delta method gives \([g'(\theta)]^2 \text{Var}(\hat\theta)\). Since \(g'(\theta)=e^\theta\), the square gives \(e^{2\theta}\), not \(e^\theta\).
This is the asymptotic variance of \(\hat{\theta}\), not of the transformed quantity \(e^{\hat{\theta}}\).
Missing the factor of \(n\) in the denominator. The asymptotic variance decreases at rate \(1/n\).
